exercises.name: The Monotone Breaker
exercises.rule: Every sentence shifts pitch or volume from the last.
exercises.why: Monotone reads as disengaged — variance is the signal of presence.

prompts.text: Walk me through what you did at work yesterday.
Explain how your morning routine usually goes.
Give me a status update on your top three projects.
Describe the last meeting you sat through.
Walk through the architecture of our auth system.
Explain how our pricing tiers are structured.
Run through last quarter's financial highlights.
Summarize the patient intake workflow end to end.
Describe how a deal moves through your sales pipeline.
Explain the curriculum sequence for an incoming cohort.
Walk through how your CI/CD pipeline is wired up.
Brief the team on this week's incident postmortem.
Read the quarterly compliance update out loud, top to bottom.
Deliver a five-minute technical onboarding to a new hire.
Narrate your entire weekly board report without notes.

exercises.name: Volume Dial
exercises.rule: Open quiet. Build to peak. Land soft.
exercises.why: Trains conscious dynamics — volume is a tool, not an accident.

prompts.text: Tell the story of how you ended up in your current job.
Recap the most memorable trip you've ever taken.
Walk me through your favorite meal, start to finish.
Tell me about a hobby that has slowly taken over your life.
Pitch our company in 90 seconds.
Tell the origin story of your startup.
Describe a turning point in your career arc.
Walk a client through the case for our flagship product.
Tell the story of a deal that nearly fell apart and how it closed.
Recap a patient case where the diagnosis surprised everyone.
Tell the story of shipping your hardest feature.
Describe the day you realized your team had finally clicked.
Deliver a toast at your closest friend's wedding.
Eulogize a mentor in three minutes.
Give a keynote about why your industry has to change — now.

exercises.name: Warmth Switch
exercises.rule: Add audible warmth on every people-related sentence.
exercises.why: Warmth in voice is what makes people-talk land as people-talk.

prompts.text: Tell me about your team.
Describe a coworker who quietly carries the team.
Tell me about someone who shaped how you work.
Talk about a friend you've kept since childhood.
Introduce your direct reports to a new exec joining the company.
Tell a customer about the engineer who built their feature.
Walk a recruit through what it's like to join your team.
Describe a nurse on your unit who patients ask for by name.
Talk about a student who completely changed how you teach.
Introduce your co-founder to a room of investors.
Tell me about the client relationship you're proudest of.
Describe your closest mentor and what they taught you.
Deliver a goodbye message to a teammate who's leaving the company.
Comfort a friend who just lost their parent.
Welcome a family whose loved one is starting hospice care.

exercises.name: Authority Voice
exercises.rule: Drop pitch on every claim of fact. No upward inflection.
exercises.why: Authority lives in the floor of your voice, not the ceiling.

prompts.text: State what you're best at, in one sentence, three times.
Tell me three things you know for certain about your craft.
Declare your role and what you own at the company.
List five facts about your product that you'd stake your career on.
Tell the board the rebrand is working.
Defend this quarter's hiring plan against pushback.
Tell the team we are not pivoting and explain why.
Brief the press on the recall — what happened, what's fixed.
Walk the audit committee through why our numbers are correct.
Deliver a diagnosis and treatment plan to the patient's family.
Explain to a skeptical client why your recommendation stands.
Tell engineering the architecture decision is final.
Address the all-hands the day after a public misstep.
Testify in front of a regulator about your firm's controls.
Tell a roomful of doctors why your trial data is conclusive.

exercises.name: Downward Landing
exercises.rule: End every statement with a falling intonation.
exercises.why: Rising terminals turn statements into questions. Land them flat or down.

prompts.text: Describe what your job actually involves day-to-day.
Tell me three things you did last weekend.
List the cities you've lived in and one detail about each.
State your name, your role, and what you're working on this week.
Summarize what your company does in five sentences.
Recap last week's standup decisions for someone who missed it.
Lay out the four pillars of your product strategy.
Describe your treatment protocol for a routine case.
Explain how you evaluate a new investment opportunity.
Outline the lesson plan you're running tomorrow.
Brief the new PM on the three workstreams under their area.
Recap the key terms of the contract you just signed.
Walk an outside auditor through how revenue recognition works here.
Deliver findings from a security incident to the exec team.
Read a list of layoff impacts to the company at an all-hands.

exercises.name: Curiosity Lift
exercises.rule: End each thought lifted — like you're still discovering it.
exercises.why: Curiosity tone invites the listener in; pre-decided tone shuts them out.

prompts.text: What's a question you've been chewing on lately?
Talk about a book or idea that's stuck with you this month.
Wonder out loud about something in your field you don't yet understand.
Describe a hobby you keep meaning to start and why.
Brainstorm three new product ideas, thinking out loud.
Walk through a customer problem you don't yet have an answer for.
Talk through a system design tradeoff you're still weighing.
Speculate about where your industry will be in ten years.
Explore why a recent patient case didn't follow the textbook.
Think out loud about a market you've never invested in before.
Reflect on a student who learns nothing like you expected.
Open a brainstorm about how to fix your team's biggest blocker.
Riff on a creative direction for the next campaign — no commitments.
Explore a belief you used to hold that you're starting to doubt.
Speculate on what your competitor is about to do, and why.

exercises.name: Conviction Floor
exercises.rule: No rising terminals on any claim of fact.
exercises.why: Builds the audible floor under your beliefs — listeners hear you mean it.

prompts.text: Tell me one thing you believe that most of your peers don't.
State the principle that guides how you do your work.
Name the rule you refuse to break, and why.
Tell me the one piece of advice you give every new hire.
Why is product-market fit the only thing that matters in early stage?
Argue for the single metric your team should be judged on.
Make the case for shipping less, more carefully.
Defend a controversial clinical decision you'd make again.
Tell investors why this is the right fund, the right vintage, the right time.
Make the case that grading destroys real learning.
Tell the team why you're killing their favorite project.
Argue for a hiring bar that everyone on your team thinks is too high.
Defend your refusal to take a meeting that everyone says you should take.
Stake your position in front of a panel of people who disagree with you.
Tell a longtime client you're firing them, and why it's the right call.

exercises.name: Pivot Tone
exercises.rule: Signal topic changes with a tonal shift, not a filler word.
exercises.why: Tone shift handles transitions that 'so anyway' and 'okay' do clumsily.

prompts.text: Tell me about your morning, your afternoon, and your evening.
Update me on the three things on your plate right now.
Cover what you ate, what you read, and what you watched yesterday.
Walk through your week: work, family, and personal goals.
Brief me on product, then on engineering, then on go-to-market.
Give updates on hiring, churn, and burn — in that order.
Cover three patients on your rounds today, each with a different status.
Move through last week's wins, this week's risks, and next week's focus.
Pitch the deal, then walk the diligence, then close on the ask.
Talk through a launch plan: messaging, channels, then measurement.
Cover three classroom incidents, each with a different tone.
Brief the team on the new feature, the deprecation, and the outage.
Move through good news, hard news, and the path forward.
Cover a celebration, a layoff, and a strategy reset in one all-hands.
Update the board on growth, on a lawsuit, and on a CFO departure.

exercises.name: Read the Room
exercises.rule: Match your tone to a stated audience cue.
exercises.why: Same words, different rooms — tone is how you actually adapt.

prompts.text: Introduce yourself — to a new neighbor over the fence.
Describe your job — to a curious eight-year-old.
Explain what you do this weekend — to your best friend, then to your boss.
Tell the story of your day — to a partner who's had a hard one.
Deliver the same product update — to engineering, then to sales.
Pitch the company — to a skeptical board.
Share quarterly results — to a team that just lost a deal.
Explain the diagnosis — to a patient, then to a fellow physician.
Walk through the investment thesis — to an LP, then to a junior analyst.
Brief the same incident — to engineers, then to legal.
Give the same lesson — to a kindergarten class, then to graduate students.
Announce the reorg — to senior leaders, then to the broader team.
Deliver the same condolence — to a friend who's grieving, then to a colleague you barely know.
Tell a hard truth — to a mentor, then to a direct report.
Defend the same decision — to a friendly reporter, then to a hostile one.
